title: Time vs Space Complexity
subtitle: Memory has its own Big O

sections.heading: Two resources, two budgets
sections.body:
Big O describes growth — but growth of *what*? So far we've talked about **time** (operations performed). The same notation describes **space** (memory used). Both matter; sometimes they trade off.

A fast algorithm that needs 10 GB of memory might be useless on a phone. A tiny-memory algorithm that takes a week is useless for real-time.

sections.heading: What counts as 'space'?
sections.body:
Space complexity measures **extra memory** the algorithm uses beyond the input. The input array doesn't count — we needed it anyway. What counts:

* New arrays, objects, maps, sets you allocate
* Recursion stack frames (a recursive function calling itself n deep uses O(n) stack)
* Local variables that grow with input size

What doesn't count: a fixed number of integer variables, loop counters, the input itself.

steps.text: Look at this function. What's the time complexity, and what's the space complexity?
Time: we loop through arr once. Set lookup/insert is O(1) on average. So total **O(n) time**.
Space: in the worst case (no duplicates) we add all n items to the Set. That's **O(n) space**.
Compare to the O(1)-space alternative: sort first, then scan adjacent pairs.
This is O(n log n) time but O(1) extra space — we modified the input in place. Classic trade-off: slower in time, frugal in space.
steps.highlight: Time: O(n log n), Space: O(1)

data.text:
Algorithm                       | Time          | Space
  --------------------------------+---------------+--------
  Array index access              | O(1)          | O(1)
  Linear search                   | O(n)          | O(1)
  Binary search (iterative)       | O(log n)      | O(1)
  Binary search (recursive)       | O(log n)      | O(log n) ← stack
  Bubble sort                     | O(n²)         | O(1)
  Merge sort                      | O(n log n)    | O(n)     ← temp arrays
  Quick sort                      | O(n log n)*   | O(log n) ← recursion
  DFS on graph (recursive)        | O(V + E)      | O(V)
  Memoized Fibonacci              | O(n)          | O(n)
  Iterative Fibonacci             | O(n)          | O(1)

sections.heading: Common space-mistake pitfalls
sections.body:
Three things developers often forget:

1. **Recursion uses stack space.** Even when there's no explicit array, every recursive call adds a frame. Deep recursion → O(depth) memory and potential stack-overflow crashes.
2. **String concatenation in loops.** In some languages, `s += x` inside a loop can be O(n²) time AND O(n²) total allocations. Use arrays + `join`.
3. **Slicing/copying inside a loop.** `arr.slice(i)` inside a loop turns an O(n) algorithm into O(n²) time and O(n²) memory churn.

questions.question: What's the space complexity of a recursive function that calls itself n times?
questions.explanation: Each recursive call adds a frame to the stack. n deep recursion → O(n) extra space.

questions.question: Which has BETTER space complexity?
options: Build a new sorted array → return it
Sort the input array in place → return it
questions.explanation: In-place sorting uses O(1) extra space; building a new array uses O(n).

questions.question: What's the space complexity of this function?
questions.explanation: The `out` array grows to length n, then `join` creates a new string of length n. O(n) extra space.

questions.question: Two algorithms both solve the problem in O(n log n) time. Algorithm A uses O(1) extra space, Algorithm B uses O(n). Which is preferable when n is huge?
options: B — extra memory speeds it up
Doesn't matter, time complexity is what counts
B — the extra space is reserved for safety
questions.explanation: Same Big O time, smaller Big O space — A is the clear winner for large inputs.
